published_date: 2026-04-24
video_id: CUfGvSf1_UA
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CUfGvSf1_UA
title: The Macro Minute: When does our Jobless Recovery theme stop being bullish for stocks?

Happy Friday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius DellaPosta, on
our Macro Minute for Friday, April 24th,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, when does
our jobless recovery theme stop being
bullish for stocks?
The answer is likely not anytime soon.
There are several macro forces that must
become consensus among global capital
allocators before the market starts to
price in the downside of the societal
disruption from AI. Number one, our
paradigm C, aka run it hot theme. Number
two, our productivity boom theme. And
number three, our source of funds theme.
Currently, consensus real GDP growth
expectations are way too low, which
implies consensus sales and earnings
growth expectations are too low as well.
Lastly, the aggressive crowding back
into AI providers and out of AI
beneficiary beneficiaries in recent
weeks signals that consensus does not
yet understand the paradigm-shifting
impact of the technology upon business
operations outside of the tech sector.
In our view, this is the next leg of the
AI trade if when the Strait of Hormuz is
reopened. And the reason we bring this
up is because we got some strong support
for our jobless recovery theme this
morning with Meta Platforms and
Microsoft pursuing workforce reduction
initiatives impacting up to 23,000
roles. Meta's targeting a 10% reduction,
roughly 8,000 employees, alongside
eliminating 6,000 open positions. Woo!
That is just like that.
Microsoft extended voluntary buyouts to
thousands of US employees with roughly
7% of its workforce eligible for the
offer. So, riddle me this.
If the companies selling us all the AI
are now cutting their head counts by,
you know, somewhere between 7, 8 to 10%
at a time,
what does that signal about where the
labor market might be in 1, 2, 3 years'
time?
Work backwards from there.
So, as always, we'll wrap it up from a
question wrap up from with a question
from our community here in our 42 Macro
Dashboard. This one's titled
Divergence Between Liquidity and
Bitcoin. It says, "On today's Macro
Minute, DD referenced a divergence
between Bitcoin and liquidity. But does
anyone remember what caused this
divergence?" So, the first thing I'll
say, just kind of taking it to a chart
to give you an indication of this
divergence, you know, you can very
clearly see that over the past kind of 6
months or so, Bitcoin has materially
diverged from our metric of global
liquidity, which is not the same as what
we've observed in the global equity
market. We've obviously seen a massive
divergence between Bitcoin and global
equities. And so, you know, from our
vantage point, I don't think we can
know. We can only speculate as to what
caused the divergence and what might
cause it to end. But from our vantage
point, what we were communicating to our
members in recent weeks and over the
past couple of months, which is
I think there's three things that you
can sort of attribute this divergence
to. Again, we don't know for sure, but
in our vantage point, we think we think
those three things are number one, there
was a lot of excess leverage being
applied to the Bitcoin asset class when
you consider the myriad of
derivative products that Michael
Saylor's firm, Strategy, applied to the
asset class. And in my opinion, I think
that just caused that that that, you
know, in the context of the risk-off
market regime that eventually developed
over the past couple of over the past 6
months or so, and particularly the
bearish crypto winter that developed in
that time frame. I think a lot of that
leverage essentially
needed to get rinsed out, for lack of a
better phrase. Number two,
and this is a little bit more
speculative than that. I think you have
to think about the the the the folks in
Silicon Valley developing the AI, the
folks in China, wherever China's version
of Silicon Valley is, developing their
AI. I think they're all smart enough to
understand that the risk of some of
these more powerful models poses to the
Bitcoin blockchain. Obviously, Treasury
Secretary Yellen and Federal Reserve
Chair Jay Powell convened a meeting of
Wall Street bank CEOs to warn them about
the risk of Anthropic's new
Amida's model from the perspective of
their security protocols. And so, I
think that's there's probably some folks
in Silicon Valley and China's version of
Silicon Valley that are developing the
technology that are probably like,
"I don't know, guys. I don't I don't
want to risk it." So, they probably cut
their Bitcoin positions or their
exposure to Bitcoin to some degree as a
function of that. And then the third
thing we would say
in terms of what
potentially is contributing to this this
divergence between liquidity and
Bitcoin, the third thing I would say is
is quantum computing.
We all know quantum computing poses a
massive security risk to every security
protocol in the world. And so,
obviously, the development of these
recursive AI models that continue to get
better, faster, like Anthropic's
Amida's, these models are going to help
accelerate the development of quantum
computing and bring forward the timing
of which we actually have live quantum
computers running and impacting global
financial markets. And so, in my
opinion, I think the confluence of those
three things were contributing to this
divergence between liquidity and
Bitcoin. So, we'll wrap it up there.
Darius Dale here presenting our Macro
Minute for Friday, April 24th, 2026.
Best of luck out there today. We'll
catch you back here on on Monday.
Cheers. Have a great weekend.
